label: B2 — Upper-Intermediate
blurb: 4 partes, 30 preguntas: opción múltiple, completar, multiple matching y entrevista.

audios.title: Part 1 · Questions 1–8
audios.intro: You will hear people talking in eight different situations. For each question, choose the best answer (A, B or C).
scripts: Question one. Woman: You started piano in January, didn't you? How's it going? Man: Slowly! The funny thing is, everyone warned me that reading music would be the nightmare, and actually I picked that up quite quickly. What I really struggle with is the timing. My teacher says I rush every single piece, and she's right.

Question two. Man: Hello, this is Sam, I have a guitar lesson with Kate on Mondays at six. Woman: Yes, I have you here. Man: I've just found out I'll be working late every Monday this month. Rather than cancel, could I switch to the Wednesday slot at the same time? Woman: Let me look. Yes, Wednesday at six is free, I'll move you now.

Question three. Teacher: I hear the same story from every adult beginner: I couldn't practise all week, so I did two hours on Sunday. I'd honestly rather you never did that. Ten minutes every day will take you further than one long session at the weekend, because your fingers and your brain need the daily repetition.

Question four. Man: You found a cello, then? Woman: Yes, second-hand, from a retired music teacher. I had it checked and the instrument itself is in beautiful condition, it sounds gorgeous. The only disappointment was the case, it's ripped along one side, so I'll have to buy a new one before I can carry it anywhere.

Question five. Girl: So what are we getting Dad for his birthday? He keeps saying he wants to learn an instrument. Boy: He fancies the drums, but can you imagine the noise through these walls? Girl: What about a keyboard? He could plug headphones in and play at midnight if he wanted, and nobody would hear a thing. Boy: Perfect, let's order one.

Question six. Woman: A message for everyone planning to visit the music school's open day this Saturday. Because of unexpected building work, the event can no longer take place at the school itself. Instead, all the taster sessions and talks will now happen online, at the same times as advertised. The link is on our website.

Question seven. Girl: Weren't you learning the violin? I saw you carrying a bass guitar yesterday. Boy: I switched last term. It wasn't that the violin was too hard, I was actually doing all right. But my mates were forming a band and they needed a bass player, and I'd much rather make music with them than practise alone in my room.

Question eight. Man: Good morning. It's about my saxophone, one of the keys has stopped closing properly. Assistant: I see. Were you thinking of trading it in? We have some lovely new models. Man: Oh no, I'd never part with it, it was my father's. I just want it working again. Assistant: Of course. Our technician can look at it today.

questions.q: What does the man find most difficult about learning the piano?
What does Sam want to do?
What is the problem with the woman's cello?
What will they buy for their father?
What has changed about the open day?
Why did the boy change instruments?
o: He wanted to play in a band

questions.q: What does the man want to do with his saxophone?
o: Exchange it for a new one

audios.title: Part 2 · Questions 9–18
audios.intro: You will hear a woman called Hannah talking about an orchestra for adult beginners. For each question, write the correct answer in the gap. Write one or two words, or a number, a date or a time.
scripts: Hello, I'm Hannah, and I'd like to tell you about the Late Starters Orchestra, a group for adults who always wanted to play music but never got the chance as children. People are often surprised we're so new. There were plans for a group like this for ages, but the orchestra itself only started in 2019, with eleven rather terrified beginners in a borrowed room. We've grown a lot since then. Today we have thirty-five members, and there's usually a short waiting list, which still amazes me. And when I say adults, I mean all ages. Plenty of members are in their thirties and forties, but our oldest member is seventy-two, and he'll happily tell you he'd never touched an instrument before his seventieth birthday. As for where we play, we did look at the concert hall in town, but the hire fees were impossible. Instead we meet in a school hall, which the head teacher lets us use for almost nothing, on the condition that we play at the school fair every summer. That seems a fair deal to us. People always ask which instruments beginners choose. You might expect the trumpet or the flute to be the favourite, but in fact most members play the violin. I think it's because you can rent one so cheaply, and because our violin tutor is such fun. I should mention our concerts, because they matter to us. We could have hired a fancy venue for our debut, but our very first concert took place at a hospital, playing for patients who couldn't get out to hear live music. It was terrifying and wonderful, and we've gone back every year since. If you're wondering about the commitment, rehearsals happen once a week and last two hours, with a tea break in the middle that some people say is the best part. Money-wise, we try to keep things accessible. Membership costs twelve pounds a month, which covers the room, the sheet music and the tutors. And here's the part I'm proudest of: thanks to donations, instruments can be borrowed from us completely free, for as long as you need one. So there's really no excuse! If you fancy joining us, the next term begins in April, and beginners are welcome to just turn up and listen first. It's never too late to start.

questions.label: The oldest member is ____ years old
The orchestra meets in a: ____ hall
The first concert took place at a: ____

audios.title: Part 3 · Questions 19–23
audios.intro: You will hear five adults talking about the main benefit of learning a musical instrument. For each speaker, choose from the list (A–H) the benefit they mention. Use each letter once. There are three extra letters you will not need.
bank: a connection to their family history
scripts: Speaker one. Woman: My job involves difficult phone calls all day, and I used to carry that tension home with me every evening. Now, the moment I sit down at the piano, it's like a tap being turned off. Even twenty minutes of scales empties my head completely. My doctor suggested exercise, but honestly, this works better than any run ever did. Speaker two. Man: My son started piano lessons at seven, and I used to get frustrated watching him stumble over the same bar again and again. Then I started learning myself, and within a month I was stumbling over exactly the same things. Now when he struggles, I know precisely how it feels from the inside. It's changed how we talk about music, and about a lot else too. Speaker three. Woman: My grandmother played the accordion at every family party back in her village, and when she died, the music died with her, or so I thought. Learning her instrument, and her songs, has been like opening a door to where my family comes from. When I play the tunes she played, I feel she's in the room. Speaker four. Man: I'm the classic person who starts things and drops them, languages, gyms, you name it. The clarinet is the first thing I've ever stuck with. Last month I passed my grade three exam, and I actually framed the certificate. People laugh, but I've never had that feeling of really earning something before. It's addictive. Speaker five. Woman: Since I took up the guitar, I've noticed something odd: names, appointments, shopping lists, they all stick better than they used to. I mentioned it to my teacher and she showed me research saying learning music genuinely trains the brain to hold on to information. I started for fun, but that's turned out to be the real prize, especially at my age.

questions.c: a connection to their family history

audios.title: Part 4 · Questions 24–30
audios.intro: You will hear an interview with a man called Daniel, a music teacher who specialises in adult beginners. For each question, choose the best answer (A, B or C).
scripts: Interviewer: Daniel, you've taught adult beginners for fifteen years. What holds adults back the most? Daniel: People expect me to say stiff fingers or a lack of time, and those are real enough. But the true obstacle is fear of getting it wrong. A child plays a wrong note and just giggles. An adult plays a wrong note and apologises, turns red, and sometimes never comes back. Until they forgive themselves for sounding bad, they can't improve. Interviewer: Do adults have any advantages over children? Daniel: A huge one, and it's not what people think. It's not discipline, plenty of my adults are as disorganised as any teenager. It's that adults know exactly why they're there. A child is often sent to lessons. An adult has chosen this, usually after wanting it for thirty years, and that motivation carries them through the difficult months. Interviewer: There's a common belief that after a certain age, it's simply too late. What do you say to that? Daniel: I hear it every week, usually from someone about to prove themselves wrong. The research is clear: the adult brain keeps its ability to learn new skills throughout life. Progress may look different, but the door never closes. My oldest starter was eighty-one, and she plays in a folk group now. Interviewer: Which instrument should an adult beginner choose? Daniel: Everyone expects a practical answer, the ukulele, because it's cheap and easy. But I always say: choose the instrument whose sound you love, even if it's supposed to be difficult. Someone who adores the cello will practise the cello. Someone talked into the recorder because it's sensible will quietly give up by Easter. Interviewer: What's your advice about practising? Daniel: Forget the heroic hour on Sunday. Attach ten minutes of practice to something you already do every day, straight after breakfast, right after the school run. Little and daily beats long and rarely, every single time. The instrument should live out of its case, somewhere you can't avoid it. Interviewer: How does adult progress typically look? Daniel: This surprises people. Adults start slower than children, the first three months can feel like walking through mud. But once the basics click, adults often overtake, because they understand structure and they practise with purpose. The ones who quit in month two never find that out, which breaks my heart a little. Interviewer: And what's next for you? Daniel: Something ambitious! Next summer we're launching a festival just for adult beginners, a whole weekend where every performer on every stage started after thirty. No prodigies allowed. I want people to see what late starters can really do. Interviewer: Daniel, thank you very much.

questions.q: According to Daniel, what is the main obstacle for adult learners?
What advantage does Daniel say adults have?
o: They know why they are learning
They have more money for lessons

questions.q: What does Daniel say about age and learning?
o: The brain can learn new skills at any age
Progress stops after a certain age
Only some adults can still learn

questions.q: Which instrument does Daniel recommend for beginners?
o: The one whose sound they love

questions.q: What practice routine does Daniel suggest?
o: Short daily sessions linked to a habit

questions.q: What does Daniel say about adults' progress?
o: It is slow at first, then speeds up
It is fast at first, then slows down
It is steady from the beginning

questions.q: What is Daniel planning for next summer?
